reasoning: Confirmed. The hidedflipRating() callback at lines 130-133 of inc/post-type.php contains exactly one statement: update_option('dflip_showratingdiv', 'no'). The AJAX hook is registered on line 91 as wp_ajax_hidedflipRating (not wp_ajax_nopriv_), so WordPress core blocks unauthenticated requests at admin-ajax.php — unauthenticated CSRF is not possible. However, there is no nonce check, no check_ajax_referer, no wp_verify_nonce, and no current_user_can anywhere in the file (confirmed by grep returning zero results). Any authenticated user tricked into visiting an attacker page that issues a POST to admin-ajax.php?action=hidedflipRating will successfully set the dflip_showratingdiv option to 'no', permanently hiding the plugin's rating-prompt admin notice. The reachability claim is accurate: authenticated CSRF only, any logged-in role suffices, is_admin() in this context is a routing check not a capability gate. Impact is cosmetically trivial — the only effect is suppressing a dismissal notice — which correctly places severity at low. No missed sanitizer or guard exists.
